_notes.file: Companion-driven debris configurations. One entry per DebrisFieldShape archetype.
_notes.editing: Pools should be terse, present-tense, and focused on consequence — match the voice of phenomena entries in narrative.json. Each archetype's spawned SystemPhenomenon draws from the four-beat structure (travelEffect / surveyQuestion / conflictHook / sceneAnchor).
_notes.minimums: Audit floor: whyHerePool, prizePool, guCharacterPool, notePool, travelEffectPool, surveyQuestionPool, conflictHookPool, sceneAnchorPool >= 5 entries each. labelPool >= 3.

mass-transfer-stream.label: Mass-Transfer Stream
whyHerePool: Material falls continuously from one star to the other across the L1 saddle, lighting the gulf between them.
The stream pulses on the binary orbital period; brightness peaks correlate with rare-element accretion.
Successive generations of harvesters have learned to time crossings against the stream's flare clock.
The donor star is shedding its outer envelope on a geological timescale; the stream is a fossil-in-progress.
Crews report the stream looks like a glowing river from a quarter-AU out.
prizePool: post-stellar heavy elements unavailable anywhere else in-system
chiral aerosols carrying single-handedness collapse signatures
fresh accretion-disc plasma sampled at the impact spot
industrial-grade isotopes dredged from a stellar interior
calibration-medium fractions only the stream produces
guCharacterPool: The L1 throat sits inside a continuous Shiab-math instability; ships that cross take a hammering they can quantify only after.
Chiral output of the stream collapses to a single handedness over hours, the cleanest single-chirality source in-system.
Bleed inside the stream reads as a continuous extreme node; long exposures kill instruments before they kill crew.
Iggygate construction across the stream would short the geometry between the two stars; no one has tried since the warning beacons appeared.
Metric storms hit on the stream's brightness cycle; the only safe windows are dim phases.

notePool: A visible river of stellar plasma arcs from one star's photosphere onto the other across the L1 saddle.
Stream brightness pulses on the binary orbital period; rare elements concentrate at the accretion hot spot.
The stream is photometrically resolvable from beyond the system; pilgrims and observatories alike track it.
Generations of harvesters have learned to read the stream's flare clock by eye.
Stream geometry shifts hour-to-hour as the donor's atmospheric layers vent.
travelEffectPool: Approach corridors are constantly redrawn; lanes inside one AU lose viability hour-to-hour.
Crossings only succeed during a brief dim-phase window; missing it costs a synodic period of waiting.
Particle flux on the stream's leading edge degrades shielding integrity within minutes of exposure.
Ship hulls accumulate isotopic markers from a single crossing; customs uses the markers to track who's been where.
X-ray spillover from the impact hot spot blinds nav sensors for hours after every transit.
surveyQuestionPool: Where is the next stable shear edge, and how long before paired flares close it?
Is the donor's mass-loss rate accelerating toward runaway, or holding?
Which photospheric layer is the stream currently sampling, and what does that say about the donor's interior?
Can the accretion hot spot's flicker frequency be predicted enough to insure a passage?
Has anything organic ever entered the stream and produced legible spectroscopy on the way through?
conflictHookPool: Salvage operators race paired flare cycles to extract rare post-stellar elements; the cycle math is contested by competing AI providers.
A chiral-monopoly cartel controls the only-known single-chirality aerosol source; new entrants are quietly disappeared.
Two rival route-weather boards publish incompatible safe-crossing windows; both sell insurance.
Gardener warning beacons appeared near the stream a generation ago; nobody has explained why, and the local interdiction council bills as if they know.
A donor-star measurement-rights treaty between three houses is up for renewal; the new terms determine who gets to publish the next century's accretion-rate forecasts.
sceneAnchorPool: A skiff threads the dim-phase shear edge; the bridge view shows the stream arcing from one sun to the other like a frozen lightning bolt.
An accretion-disc refinery rides the rim of the hot spot, hauls running at maximum cool.
Three pilgrim transports queue in low parking, waiting for the next stable observation window.
A customs cutter scans an arriving freighter's hull for stream-isotope markers and finds three that do not match the manifest.
A wake-reader sits cross-legged on a glassed observation deck, listening to the stream and saying nothing.

common-envelope-shell.label: Common-Envelope Ejecta Shell
whyHerePool: Centuries ago this binary ejected its shared envelope in a single event; the shell is still expanding outward.
Settlements inside the shell were laid before anyone realized the system carried this scar.
The shell's inner front is still photoionized by the stripped core; some nights it still glows.
Heavy elements ejected from one star's interior salt every body inside the shell.
The natural transponder-scrambling of the shell makes traffic inside slightly off-the-books by default.
prizePool: heavy-element-enriched regolith on every body inside the shell
first-wave salvage cached when the local archive collapsed
concentric chiral assay readings older than any current government
ionization-resistant alloys distilled from envelope dust
untracked freight that drifted into the shell during the silent decades
guCharacterPool: The shell is a fossilized metric storm; bleed inside reads as scar-class, occasionally re-igniting at the inner front.
Concentric handedness bands record the event the way tree rings record summers; a careful assay reads the whole history.
The metric inside is unusually flat — the storm passed, and what remains is the silence after.
Outer-shell anchor points are some of the quietest legal Iggygate sites in the reachable region.
Photoionization at the inner front still pulses with the orbital remnant; clocks run subtly fast there.

notePool: A near-spherical shell of dust and gas surrounds the binary, decades to millennia old.
Inner-shell ionization makes the local sky glow faintly pink on the night side of every body inside.
Settlements inside the shell were laid before anyone understood what the shell was; their charters predate the diagnosis.
The shell scrambles long-range transponders; freight inside is harder to track than freight outside.
Heavy-element enrichment in shell-interior planets makes them metallurgically valuable and politically contested.
travelEffectPool: Crossing the shell is slow; low-density dust still abrades hulls.
Transponder reliability drops by half inside the shell, by half again at the inner ionization front.
Long-range communication blackouts of three to fifteen minutes are routine inside the shell.
Dust accretion on cooler instruments forces a recalibration every transit.
Stable approach lanes are aligned to the original ejection axis; off-axis crossings cost extra fuel.
surveyQuestionPool: Which star contributed which material, and when did the envelope event happen?
Is the shell still expanding, or has it stalled against the local interstellar medium?
What evacuated installation is buried in the inner regolith of which body?
Whose charter predates the ejection, and does the paperwork still hold?
Are the inner-front photoionization pulses consistent with a single event, or two?
conflictHookPool: A salvage court claim around a shell-buried installation could overturn current concessions held by three rival houses.
The current factions inherited the shell; an heir surfaces every decade with paperwork older than any of them.
A first-wave compliance audit is suddenly relevant because the shell's quiet zone hosts a Gardener-watched site nobody acknowledges.
Heavy-element extraction rights inside the shell are being renegotiated; the previous treaty assumed the shell was inert.
Two settlement councils dispute which side of the envelope they originally came from; the case turns on chiral assay of buried tools.
sceneAnchorPool: A dust-veiled twilight system where every horizon glows faintly pink and the stars look filtered.
A surveyor scrapes paint from a buoy that is older than any local record.
An ionization-front auroral display silently lights a settlement's morning side once per local week.
A clerk in a sealed records vault flips through paper charters that predate the binary's current names.
A salvage crew opens a long-buried hatch and finds the air inside is still pressurized.

polar-ring.label: Polar Circumbinary Ring
whyHerePool: A ring of dust and pebbles orbits the close binary perpendicular to the orbital plane, a tilted crown above and below the suns.
The polar ring sits in the metric-quiet band between the binary's rhythmic tides; a station hovering above it sees a far cleaner local geometry than one in-plane.
The ring's age does not match the system's other belts; isotopes read older, as if captured from elsewhere.
Every binary orbital period, the ring eclipses both stars once from the perspective of any in-plane body — a recurring sky event the locals plan their calendars around.
Dust transport along the ring follows secular resonance, not Keplerian flow; mining requires custom navigation.
prizePool: rare metallicity-mismatched grains hinting at capture from a vanished primordial disk
uncontested astrometric vantage above the binary plane
shielded observation real estate that no in-plane sensor can match
concentrated dust at the ring's two in-plane intersection nodes
polar-aligned cargo storage immune to the binary's flare cones
guCharacterPool: Bleed is moderate but eerily clean — a metric eye between the binary's two beat patterns.
Chiral concentration is low except at the equatorial intersection points, where two gradients meet at right angles.
Anchor candidacy is high for stations seated above the binary plane; the polar quiet band is a rare Iggygate site.
The intersection points are pinch hazards, not anchor sites; anything that lingers there sweats matter-phase instability.
The polar geometry favors observerse anchors that depend on metric stillness rather than mass loading.

notePool: A perpendicular ring of dust and small bodies orbits the binary at right angles to the orbital plane.
The ring eclipses both suns once per binary period, casting a recurring false winter on circumbinary planets.
Two in-plane intersection nodes concentrate the ring's mass; everywhere else the band is thin.
Stations seated above or below the binary plane sit in the cleanest local metric in the system.
The ring's composition reads as foreign to local stellar metallicity — older, captured, unaccounted for.
travelEffectPool: In-plane traffic ignores the ring entirely except during the twice-per-period eclipse cone.
High-inclination surveys must cross the ring; dust counts blank sensors for fifteen to thirty minutes per crossing.
Polar-vector approaches into the system are the fastest path to the inner zone, but only one syndicate publishes the chart.
Cargo storage above the plane saves fuel for any operation that needs astrometric stability.
Equatorial intersection-node transits are a known cause of pinchdrive misjumps.
surveyQuestionPool: Where did the ring's older material come from?
Which intersection node is currently denser, and how is that shifting?
Is the ring stable on the system's age, or is it draining inward toward the keep-out?
Could a chiral assay date the ring's capture event to within a century?
What does the ring's eclipse cycle do to circumbinary planet biospheres over millennia?
conflictHookPool: The polar shadow station controls astrometric truth for the whole system; whoever holds it sells the calibration.
A survey bureau's polar-passage chart is contested by three competing operators; lives depend on which one is right.
An interdiction picket holds the polar approach; the local government insists it is a customs station and not a Gardener proxy.
Inheritance of a polar shadow platform is in dispute because the original charter pre-dates the local government's recognition of polar real estate.
A sensor-truth cartel publishes the only widely accepted ring-density forecasts and quietly suppresses competing data.
sceneAnchorPool: A station hangs perfectly still inside a glittering halo while both suns blaze sideways across the windows.
Surveyors watch a settlement's hazard data being rewritten in real time as the ring rotates overhead.
An eclipse cone slides across a circumbinary planet's dayside, dimming everything for three hours by the clock.
A polar-route smuggler exits the ring at the dense intersection node, escorted by a tug that knows the dust storms by name.
A geometer drops a probe through the polar shadow and listens to it report back as if from a quieter universe.

trojan-camp.label: Binary Trojan Camp
whyHerePool: Two tadpole-shaped swarms ride sixty degrees ahead of and behind the secondary, captured planetesimals librating around the Lagrange points.
Trojan habitats are intrinsically stable; the same orbit that holds the rocks holds the cities.
Long-duration crews report L4-harvested ice cores favor one chirality and L5 the other by about one part in eight, a difference that matters at refinery scale.
The Trojan camps have hosted continuous habitation since before any current jurisdiction was named.
Whoever holds the L4/L5 holdings of the more massive star sets the calibration-medium price for the whole pair.
prizePool: primordial planetesimals mixed with cached storage hulks
asteroid-tunnel-grade ore on long-term claim ledgers
single-handedness ice cores from one of the two Lagrange wells
co-orbital cargo caches outside any in-plane customs jurisdiction
ancient claim records held by families older than the local government
guCharacterPool: Bleed is faint and reliable — Trojans sit at stable equilibria, so bleed there sits on the useful end of the intensity band.
L4 and L5 run opposite handed; long-duration crews tell each other apart by their chiral signatures.
Anchor candidacy is excellent; listening posts, observerse anchor stations, and witness-core vaults end up here disproportionately.
The Trojan camps are the closest thing to honest real estate a binary system offers.
Iggygate construction at either Trojan is documented and broadly stable; the throat sits inside a quiet metric well.

notePool: Two co-orbital swarms of captured planetesimals ride the binary's L4 and L5 points, each a tadpole-shaped libration cloud.
Asteroid-tunnel cities and family-trust refineries inhabit the larger rocks; smaller ones are claim-marked but unworked.
The two camps run measurably different chiralities — refineries specialize accordingly.
Approach to the Trojans is straightforward in principle but politically dense; every approach lane has a contracted operator.
A missed Trojan-clear window costs a synodic period of waiting, no matter the ship's class.
travelEffectPool: Trojan clouds force ships through monitored gaps shared by dust and salvage drones.
Inter-camp transfers must wait for a synodic alignment that recurs predictably but rarely.
Cargo manifests crossing between L4 and L5 are scrutinized for chiral-signature drift.
The leading and trailing cones obscure deep-sky observation for a measurable fraction of each binary year.
Smuggler routes thread the outer tadpole boundary where rocks are sparser but ephemerides are unreliable.
surveyQuestionPool: Which clusters are primordial, and which conceal storage hulks or contraband habitats?
How much of the difference between L4 and L5 chirality is original, and how much is the result of centuries of selective refining?
Is the libration period drifting, or stable on the system's age?
Which family's claim ledger is the oldest legitimate record in the camp?
What did the captured planetesimals form around before they fell into this orbit?
conflictHookPool: Mining combines and traffic authorities dispute whether the swarm is resource or hazard.
Two old families claim the same Trojan-rock charter under different generations of the same treaty.
A calibration-medium price war between the L4 and L5 refineries threatens to crater the system's whole economy for a decade.
A storage hulk inside one tadpole is rumored to hold a witness core; three syndicates have offered to buy the rock under different names.
A new Iggygate proposal at L5 conflicts with an existing anchor at L4; the legal question is whether the binary can support both.
sceneAnchorPool: The Lagrange horizon looks solid with stones blinking claimant beacons.
Two camps of asteroids 60 degrees ahead and behind a sun, sharing the same orbit but never meeting.
A family-trust refinery drops a chirality-sealed vial into a courier's hand; the seal will be opened in another camp seven months from now.
A blinker-field of claimant lights against a starscape where two suns hold steady relative positions while the rest of the sky rotates.
An anchor crew tunes an Iggygate throat at L4 while their counterpart at L5 listens for the harmonics.

inner-pair-halo.label: Hierarchical Inner-Pair Halo
whyHerePool: Debris pumped by the close inner pair is contained by the distant outer star, forming a layered halo with resonance-stable bands.
The halo's chemistry betrays the inner pair's metallicity in concentric layers, like a planetary nebula seen from inside.
Outer-star planets sit comfortably beyond the halo; inner-pair operations sit just inside the keep-out — the halo is the buffer.
Mean-motion resonance with the outer companion carves natural gaps through the halo; these are the only stable trade routes.
The whole inner halo is high-military-value real estate; every faction in a hierarchical triple has an opinion about it.
prizePool: single-handedness chiral ore concentrated at predictable orbital phases
resonance-stable real estate at the halo gaps
primordial inner-pair condensate too pumped to settle elsewhere
salvage from the centuries of attempted trans-halo crossings
anchor-mass auction lots for proposed Iggygates at resonance nodes
guCharacterPool: Bleed is rich and rhythmic; the halo lives inside two beat patterns at once, with major nodes at the resonance crossings.
Chiral pumping sorts material by handedness over long timescales, producing concentrated reserves at predictable orbital phases.
Anchor candidacy is very high at specific resonance-stable points and very low elsewhere; the cartography of the halo is itself the prize.
The 'major bleed economy' the canonical hierarchical-triple line points at lives here.
An Iggygate built on a halo resonance node carries one of the highest stability ratings in the reachable universe; building one is correspondingly expensive and contested.

labelPool: Containment annulus of the inner pair
notePool: An annulus of debris between the inner-pair keep-out and the outer companion's orbit, pumped by the inner pair and contained by the outer star.
Resonance with the outer companion carves natural gaps through the halo; gap stations dominate inter-tier traffic.
Halo composition is layered by inner-pair stellar wind and ejection history; chiral assay reads the layers like book pages.
Outer-star planets are normal civilian real estate; inner-pair installations are industrial; the halo is contested in between.
A two-tier economy operates around the halo — inner-pair workers and outer-star civilians mediated by halo gate-keepers.
travelEffectPool: Inter-tier transits must thread one of the resonance gaps; off-resonance crossings are not survivable.
Gap-station tolls scale with cargo mass and political season.
The halo's pumped material occasionally throws a planetesimal inward on a Kozai-like cycle; every century or so a hazard alert closes the gaps.
Sensor signatures inside the halo are scrambled by resonance harmonics; navigation falls back to anchored beacons.
Customs picket boats inside the halo gaps cycle through patrol seasons that align with the outer-companion year.
surveyQuestionPool: Which resonance gaps are stable on the system's age, and which are drifting?
How is the chiral layering of the halo evolving as the inner pair ages?
Was the outer companion an original system member or a captured passer-by?
Where in the halo could a new Iggygate sit without disturbing an existing one?
What did the last hazard cycle teach about the halo's pumping rate?
conflictHookPool: Inner-pair workers and outer-star civilians dispute the halo gap toll schedule, mediated by gate-keepers whose authority is unclear.
An old gap-station's charter has lapsed; three operators claim continuity, two with weapons.
A new anchor mass auction at a resonance node is in dispute because two prospective gates would interfere with each other.
Strikebreaker history at the inner-pair industrial belt resurfaces; the halo's gap station logs the case file.
A halo composition study would prove or disprove the outer companion's capture origin, with major political consequences for the system's chartering.
sceneAnchorPool: A gap station hangs in a clear lane; halo dust scrapes against the outer hull and the inner-pair flares light it from below.
A geometer at an anchor-mass site listens for the harmonics of the halo's two beat patterns crossing.
An inner-pair refinery's morning shift watches the halo gap close on the customs schedule and waits.
An outer-star civilian liner threads the same gap a hundred years after the last family member's first transit.
A negotiator from the inner-pair labor council sits across a gap-station table from a Gardener-compliance auditor; neither blinks.

kozai-scattered-halo.label: Kozai-Lidov Scattered Halo
whyHerePool: A distant third companion drives the local debris into chaotic high-inclination orbits on a Kozai-Lidov cycle measured in centuries.
Bodies in this halo are on chaotic, high-inclination, eccentric paths; conventional ephemerides fail past a few decades.
The halo has been quiet for the last two hundred years; the previous tenants left because that stopped being true; the next tenants are due.
Whoever owns the cycle clock owns the harvest; nobody has a working clock everybody agrees on.
Material here cycles between dormant and lethal; the gap between is the only time anyone works.
prizePool: high-inclination ice cores from the dormant phase between pump cycles
rare metric-active fragments tossed inward by Kozai oscillations
outer-system primordial material delivered cheaply at every perihelion swing
salvage from the last cycle's failed operations
century-scale predictive contracts for the next cycle
guCharacterPool: Bleed is pulsed; quiet for ten thousand years, a major shear zone for a thousand, then quiet again.
Every Kozai cycle re-shuffles handedness; the halo is one of the few stable sources of both chiralities in measurable quantity.
Anchor candidacy is poor for permanent structures, excellent for seasonal operations; a refinery designed to leave on a five-century clock can do well.
Whoever holds the clock on a Kozai halo holds the harvest; the clocks themselves are contested instruments.
Pinchdrive misjump rates spike during pump cycles; routine traffic is suspended for the duration.

notePool: A halo of bodies on chaotic high-inclination orbits pumped by a distant third star.
Orbits cannot be back-propagated past about ten thousand years; the chaos is mathematical, not technological.
Bodies cycle between quiet and pumped phases on a Kozai timescale of centuries to millennia.
Each pump phase delivers a wave of inward-falling debris that lasts for generations.
The next pump phase is the subject of a forecasting industry of competing AI providers.
travelEffectPool: Conventional ephemerides fail; ships subscribe to a chaos-forecast service or trade at risk.
Storm-chaser convoys follow individual bodies for months at a time, never settling.
Pump-phase weather closes the halo to ordinary traffic for years on end.
Three certified ephemerides for the same body can disagree by AU-scale margins; insurance underwriters arbitrate.
Inertial dampers strain inside the halo; long crossings require unusual maintenance.
surveyQuestionPool: When did the last pump cycle peak, and what is the next forecast?
Which bodies are quiet long enough to host a temporary camp?
How does the halo's chiral distribution vary with phase?
What buried instrument from the last cycle is still recoverable?
Whose chaos model has the best track record over the previous three cycles?
conflictHookPool: The navigation guild that sells halo-passage forecasts is selling probabilistic guesses; failures are profitable for the firm that wrote the policy.
Different certified AIs give different halo predictions; choosing one closes off the others.
A late-cycle harvest is racing the next pump phase; multiple operators are running the same calendar against each other.
A failed Kozai forecast caused a fleet-scale disaster a decade ago; the case is still in the courts and someone is paying to keep it there.
An inheritance dispute hinges on which heir signed leases for the next cycle's harvest, when the cycle's start was misforecast.
sceneAnchorPool: A nav shack lit by three overlaid hazard projections; the dispatcher draws straws with the captain over which one to plot against.
A storm-chaser convoy ties down on an asteroid that will be ejected in eighteen months by their own forecast.
An astronomer-priest reads the halo's chaos signature like a divination; the result will be sold by the syndicate her order signed for.
A salvage crew uncovers a refinery from the previous pump cycle and finds the records intact.
A wake-reader listens to a freshly arrived body and says the bleed in it is older than the system.

hill-sphere-capture-cone.label: Hill-Sphere Capture Cone
whyHerePool: A companion star sweeps the system's edge, capturing dust and weakly bound rocks in its trailing wake.
Captured material settles into chaotic, often retrograde orbits for years to decades before being ejected.
Stations on captured bodies live on a clock; once your asteroid is ejected, you go with it.
Inventory in the cone changes between surveys; last year's three asteroids are gone, there are seven new ones now.
Smugglers love the cone's one-way valve behavior; legitimate traders do not.
prizePool: interstellar dust the companion has dragged in from outside the system
weakly bound planetesimals from the primary's outer halo, now in temporary custody
ancient ejecta from past common-envelope events captured in transit
untracked freight that drifted into the cone during the silent decades
title-portable real estate that travels with the rock
guCharacterPool: Bleed is chronic and moderate — the Hill-sphere edge is a continuous low-grade metric tide.
Material constantly re-mixes; long-term residents report mild but persistent chiral contamination even with clean stock.
Anchor candidacy is tolerable but not great; the cone is not stable enough for an Iggygate but is stable enough for a working bleed harvester.
The cone is working-class harvester country; independent captains, eighteen-month leases, calibration-medium refineries the size of a hab module.
Wake-readers do good business at the cone's edge; the geometry there talks back.

notePool: A trailing wake of debris the companion star captures and re-ejects on its orbital schedule.
Captured bodies linger for years or decades before being thrown back out by the same companion that caught them.
Inventory inside the cone is fundamentally non-stationary; charts age in months, not years.
Salvage courts hate the cone; title transfers with the rock.
The cone's leading bow accumulates dust the companion swept from the interstellar medium; the trailing tail carries stolen primary-system material.
travelEffectPool: Drift in during one orbital phase, get reliably ejected on a later phase, no propulsion needed.
Smugglers use the cone as a deniable rendezvous; legitimate freight avoids it for the same reason.
Continuous astrogation surveys are needed to map the cone's current contents; published charts are out of date the day they print.
Hull abrasion from the cone's swept dust is unusually high; coatings have to be specified for it.
Customs cutters patrol the cone's neck, where every captured body has to pass through to leave.
surveyQuestionPool: What is currently in the cone, and when will each thing be ejected?
Which captured rocks carry isotopes that didn't originate in this system?
How long has any given installation been in the cone, and where is it going?
Whose claims on which currently-captured bodies are still valid?
Has anyone maintained a continuous registry across more than one ejection cycle?
conflictHookPool: An old claim filed on a body that left the cone two ejection cycles ago is now legally on a rock somewhere else entirely.
A salvage court refuses to recognize cone-resident claims; the court's own building is on a captured body that will leave next year.
A smuggler-port mayor argues for cone-resident voting rights; the legal question is whether the mayor's office is still in the system after the next ejection.
Two ore concerns dispute which of them filed first on a body whose ephemerides put it in different jurisdictions at different times.
A free-captain court rules on inheritance cases involving cone-captured assets; the ruling's binding scope is itself disputed.
sceneAnchorPool: A salvage crew tethered to an asteroid watches the binary slowly tilt overhead, with a countdown on the navigator's panel marked 'ejection window.'
A wake-reader sits on a cone-edge platform and listens to the new captures, naming each one before its first survey report.
A smuggler port's whole population packs up over a season because their host body is leaving.
A customs cutter scans an outbound captured rock and finds three cargo holds it didn't expect.
A free-captain court meets on whatever rock is most central this quarter; the lawyers complain but show up anyway.

exocomet-swarm.label: Resonance-Pumped Exocomet Swarm
whyHerePool: Long-period comets pumped by binary resonance return through the habitable zone on a predictable multi-millennial cadence.
The swarm has the same fifty or so comets every cycle; the locals name them, track them, plan around them.
Civilizations old enough remember the last apparition as a religious event or an apocalypse-myth.
The system's route weather has an era, not just a day; quiet centuries and rich centuries alternate.
Insurance products exist for comet-crossing seasons, written by underwriters whose grandparents wrote the last cycle's terms.
prizePool: frozen original-system material delivered at periapsis without anyone needing to fly out for it
chiral comet ice favored by the swarm's resonance pump
predictable harvest schedules a religious order can finance
spectroscopic samples of outer-system reservoir material
century-old apparition calendars that still hold against current observation
guCharacterPool: Bleed arrives with each apparition; nodes that move, on a schedule.
Long-period sorting collapses comets toward single handedness over megayears; experienced observers predict the next apparition's chirality from this one's.
Anchor candidacy is irrelevant for the swarm itself; very relevant for the habitable-zone planets the swarm crosses.
Settled worlds in resonance-pumped systems build their long-term religious calendars and their freight schedules around comet arrivals.
Gardener-fear cults love these systems; the calendar is older than any current human authority and the apparitions arrive on it.

notePool: A swarm of long-period comets pumped by binary resonance returns on a predictable multi-millennial cycle.
The same comets recur; the locals name and track them.
Each apparition delivers measurable volatiles to inner planets, salting biospheres and economies alike.
Outer-system reservoirs feed the swarm; cycle length depends on the binary's exact resonance.
A rich apparition period is the most contested commercial event in the system's century-scale calendar.
travelEffectPool: Outer-approach lanes are dangerous during rich apparition periods, easy during quiet ones.
Comet-shadow ambushes are a recurring tactical reality during inbound periods.
Long-haul freight routes are scheduled around the next apparition window.
Insurance rates for inner-system traffic shift by orders of magnitude across the cycle.
Apparition seasons close certain ports outright for the duration.
surveyQuestionPool: Which comet is leading the next apparition season?
Has the cycle clock drifted since the last well-documented event?
Who owns the right to sample any given comet this time around?
What previously-undelivered volatile chemistry will arrive this cycle?
Does the chirality forecast for this apparition match the religious calendar prediction?
conflictHookPool: The comet is six years out and three families are already arguing about who gets the chiral assay. The last one made three families and ended two.
Sampling rights for the next apparition are held by a defunct corporation; six successor claims are in court.
A religious order's calendar predicts the apparition will deliver an apocalyptic chiral signature; an insurance combine is lobbying for a counter-narrative.
Two harvest fleets are racing to be the first to land a probe; the first probe back sets the cycle's prevailing price.
A Gardener-fear cult buries its dead in advance of every apparition; the burial sites are increasingly disputed real estate.
sceneAnchorPool: A cold dawn at an observatory watching the first of the season come in — a single bright streak after generations of waiting.
An astronomer-priest reads the apparition forecast to a congregation that has not seen this comet in three lifetimes.
A salvage fleet drops sample probes one after another into a single body whose name is on every century-old map.
An insurance adjuster signs off on a cycle's policy after reading the previous cycle's claim ledger in one sitting.
An apparition calendar in a sealed glass case dates its last entry to before the local government existed.

accretion-bridge.label: Accretion Bridge
whyHerePool: A narrow, optically visible gas and dust corridor connects two close stars; tighter and more coherent than a diffuse stream.
The system is a beacon from light-years away; pilgrim traffic, observatory traffic, and Sol-interdiction scrutiny all converge on it.
The bridge has chemistry that doesn't match either star alone; surveys keep returning the same impossible spectrum.
Locals say the two stars are holding hands across the thread; whatever you drop into it goes somewhere there is no chart for.
The Gardener has dropped warning beacons near the bridge every time someone proposes building a structure inside it.
prizePool: spectroscopically unique mixed-photosphere chemistry sampled near the footpoints
tourist and pilgrim traffic measured in the millions per local year
scarcity rents on the one or two viewing platforms cleared for use
exotic decay products that drift inward from the bridge's middle
covert deniable disposal services rumored to operate at the bridge's center
guCharacterPool: The bridge is itself a bleed corridor; the geometry between the two stars goes thin along its length and along no other axis.
Metric stress is extreme along the long axis and near-zero perpendicular; crews who learn to enter perpendicular survive.
Anchor candidacy at in-bridge sites is zero; at the paired footpoints on each star's photosphere, near-uniquely high.
An Iggygate across the bridge could in principle short the geometry between the two stars; none has been attempted, and the Gardener has dropped warning beacons every time someone considered.
Smugglers' grandparents tell stories about the bridge; what gets dropped in it does not come back.

notePool: A visibly luminous gas-and-dust corridor connects two close stars, narrow and coherent.
The bridge has chemistry from neither star alone; the spectrum is the system's signature.
Pilgrim traffic, observatory traffic, and interdiction scrutiny converge on the bridge's two endpoints.
The bridge is a chronic, navigable, scheduled hazard; the only safe transits are perpendicular to its long axis.
A folk reputation has built up around the bridge: things dropped into its center are not recovered.
travelEffectPool: Crossing parallel to the bridge is forbidden; perpendicular crossings are tolerated and tightly logged.
Long-range communications garble inside the bridge's plasma envelope.
Hull radiation hardening must exceed normal stellar-photosphere rating to survive a brief bridge transit.
Pilgrim transports follow a low-parking lane that takes a season longer than a direct path.
Stealth ops favor the bridge's noise floor; legitimate traffic avoids it for the same reason.
surveyQuestionPool: What's in the bridge's middle, and why does the spectrum read the same year over year?
Is the bridge a natural feature or a GU-layer phenomenon being mistaken for one?
Which star is currently donating which elements to the bridge?
Why do Gardener warning beacons appear in the same locations every generation?
Has any object dropped into the bridge ever been recovered?
conflictHookPool: Two pilgrim orders dispute access to the only cleared viewing platform; the platform's structural certification is being renewed and the certifying body is bribable.
Sol-interdiction inspectors visit on a fixed cycle; the local government schedules its more contested business around the visits.
A smuggling combine offers deniable disposal at the bridge's center; the price has risen every decade since the practice was first rumored.
Observatories from three jurisdictions argue over publication rights for the bridge's spectroscopic surveys.
An attempted Iggygate at one bridge footpoint was abandoned a generation ago; the wreckage is still there, and reopening the project is a recurring proposal.
sceneAnchorPool: A pilgrim transport docks at a viewing platform; the bridge fills half the sky like a frozen lightning bolt that has been burning quietly for a million years.
A surveyor at the perpendicular crossing line photographs the bridge from one of the only angles the certifying body allows.
A smuggler boat skips a payload over the bridge's middle and the crew refuses to watch where it lands.
A Gardener-warning beacon arrives at one of the bridge's footpoints; nobody around it speaks for an hour after.
An astronomer publishes the bridge's spectrum every generation and every generation it reads exactly the same.

gardener-cordon.label: Gardener-Quarantine Cordon
whyHerePool: A geometry-perfect ring of debris marks a perimeter the Gardener does not want crossed; nobody built it, and everyone knows what built it.
The cordon's material is geometrically locked, all the same handedness, all aligned to the same metric — the only known material with that signature is post-strike residue.
Inside the cordon: unknown. Outside the cordon: normal local conditions. The warning beacons mean it.
Insurance, customs, navigation, religion — every part of the route economy bends around a Gardener cordon.
No human installation has been permitted to survive on a cordon for more than a few years.
prizePool: samples of post-strike residue material the Gardener leaves behind
perimeter cartography sold by professional cordon-mappers
interdiction-compliance certification rents for adjacent traffic
salvage from prior installations that did not survive the Gardener's notice
religious-pilgrimage access fees to the outer perimeter
guCharacterPool: The cordon material is flat, dead, scar-class; outside the cordon, local conditions resume.
Cordon residue carries a forced single chirality and a locked metric; no other material in the reachable universe matches.
The cordon is itself an anchor for something nobody human understands; no installation built on one has lasted.
Detour at any cost is the canonical rule; insurance, customs, religion, navigation all bend around the cordon.
The thing that built the cordon is still watching it; the warning beacons confirm this on a regular schedule.

notePool: A ring of debris with the unmistakable signature of a surgical-strike edge case marks a perimeter the Gardener does not want crossed.
The cordon material is geometrically locked; all the same handedness, all the same metric alignment, no other source.
Warning beacons arrive at predictable intervals; the source is unknown but reliable.
No human installation on a cordon has survived more than a few years.
Religious orders treat the cordon as sacred; interdiction-compliance auditors treat it as bright-line forbidden.
travelEffectPool: Standard navigation forbids crossing; every commercial routing system has the cordon hard-coded.
Detour costs are absorbed by the route economy without question; cordons price in below the noise.
Communication near the cordon experiences periodic interference matching the warning-beacon schedule.
Insurance underwriters refuse coverage for any operation within a defined radius.
Sensor data inside the cordon's nominal volume returns null; instruments report 'nothing,' which is exactly the wrong kind of nothing.
surveyQuestionPool: What was here before the strike?
Why has the cordon's geometry not decayed in centuries of observation?
What is the warning-beacon schedule actually warning?
Why does this site exist, when others nearby do not?
Has anything ever crossed the cordon and returned with legible logs?
conflictHookPool: An interdiction-compliance auditor finds an unlogged installation just inside the cordon's outer edge; the question is whose responsibility it is to remove it.
A religious order petitions for pilgrimage access to the cordon's outer perimeter; the regional government cannot decide whether to grant or deny without provoking the Gardener.
A salvage syndicate found the wreckage of a prior installation just outside the cordon; the salvage rights are contested by descendants of the original builders.
A scientific expedition proposes to sample cordon residue; the proposal has been re-submitted every decade for a century without approval.
A smuggling combine routes through the cordon-adjacent traffic corridor for the political cover; the corridor's customs station is overwhelmed.
sceneAnchorPool: The ring is geometry-perfect, captain. We did not build it. The thing that built it is still watching it.
A warning beacon arrives at a buoy at the cordon's outer edge; the buoy's onboard chronometer is the only system in the region that doesn't reset.
A pilgrim approaches the cordon's nominal perimeter, kneels, and reads aloud from a list of names the cordon is rumored to commemorate.
An interdiction-compliance auditor reads a logbook entry from a hundred years ago that begins 'standard approach' and ends mid-sentence.
A salvage crew picks through wreckage just outside the cordon and finds a tool whose serial number predates the local government.
